title: EMS Program Review Fall 2024 Draft 1 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous
Comprehensive Program Review Two-Year Cycle
EMS Program Overview
Kirk Thomsen, EMS Director
December 2024
I. Description of the Program:
The Emergency Medical Services (EMS) program consists of CPR, Public Safety-
First Aid, Emergency Medical Responder (EMR), Emergency Medical
Technician (EMT), Paramedic, and Bachelor’s degree in Paramedicine.
a) Organization
Full Time Faculty:
EMS Director/Coordinator- Kirk Thomsen
Paramedic Instructor-Theresa Gowan, EMT-P
Adjunct Faculty:
Constance Duffle, EMT-P
Randy Boyd, EMT-P
Jae Johnson EMT-P
Brian Witherell, EMT-P
Sean Prouty EMT-P
Scott Healy, RN
Administrative Support:
Administrative Assistant- Monique Gonzalez
Administrative Assistant – Anne Marie Acord
Part Time Faculty for this Review Period:
Multiple Expert Instructional Aids (EIA)
b) Primary purpose:
The Emergency Medical Services (EMS) Program trains students to provide immediate
care to the critically ill and injured. Paramedics and Emergency Medical Technicians (EMT)
determine the nature and extent of illness or injury, provide advanced life support, communicate
with other emergency medical services personnel and transport patients to the hospital. This
program meets or exceeds all state and national guidelines for paramedic training. Students who
successfully complete the requirements for these programs will be prepared for the National
Registry examination leading to State certification or license.

• Certification in Public Safety-First Aid (Title 22)
• EMT and Paramedic course completion to allow for National Registry testing.

f) Curriculum Current and needs based:

-- 4 of 9 --

title: EMS Program Review Fall 2024 Draft 1 (Part 2)
text:
The Federal Department of Transportation keeps the required curriculum updated. When new
curriculum requirements are updated we adjust accordingly. Even though we are in the state of
California, we teach the students the national scope of practice which prepares them for the
National Registry of EMTs testing, as well as opportunities in other states.
Course Evaluation Data:
Per accreditation requirements, student satisfaction surveys are required for Paramedic cohorts, clinical
facilities, and instruction. Additionally, surveys by employers of new graduates are required six months
after graduation. Furthermore, annual reports are required to be submitted to our accreditation agency
with student success rates regarding course completion and National Registry testing.
g) Class Breakdown:
• EMS 0950- CPR for the professional Rescuer

• EMS 1001- Emergency Medical Technician

• EMS 9060- Emergency Medical Technician Refresher
• EMS 9065- Emergency Medical Responder
• EMS 9066- Emergency Medical Responder Refresher
• EMS 9080- First Aid for Public Safety Personnel Title 22
• EMS 9081- Advanced First Aid
• EMS 9082- Community First Aid

-- 5 of 9 --

title: EMS Program Review Fall 2024 Draft 1 (Part 3)
text:
II. External Factors with Significant Impact
a) Budgetary constraints or opportunities:
Balancing the needs of the community for Emergency Medically trained
personnel and balancing the enrollment needs of the College of the Siskiyous, can be a
significant challenge. Since the target audience of the entry level classes such as EMR
and EMT is typically fire service personnel. In years when California wildfires extend
into November it is challenging to fill a class in the Fall semester. The Paramedic
program starts in October just to avoid this conflict. The Paramedic Program is one of
the more expensive programs within College of the Siskiyous. We are challenged to
balance the costs versus benefits to the community and to each prospective student for the
opportunity to gain an esteemed profession that can provide tremendous satisfaction and
a valuable service to the community. Local agencies within the region depend upon the
training of EMT’s and Paramedics to maintain their staffing and quality of care. College
of the Siskiyous graduates are regarded as some of the best EMS personnel, they are
sought for employment by agencies not only in Siskiyou County but throughout the Far
North region of California and into Southern Oregon.
b) Cooperation with other institutions:
Mendocino College is working with us to provide the satellite location for the
Paramedic Program. This partnership is in its second year and is very successful, almost
doubling the size of our Paramedic Program. Other institutions have reached out to us
about doing the same thing and we are evaluating them individually, to not reduce our
Weed campus enrollment while expanding the program size.
III. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
Overall, program outcomes indicate that most students who enroll complete the courses
successfully. Attrition is generally quite low in the Paramedic Program. Changes in
staffing and scheduling have helped make the success rate rise.
IV. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
Pass rates for the Paramedic Program National Registry tests are most frequently used
measures for the Paramedic and EMT programs. Accredited Paramedic Programs track
and report outcome measures annually to the Committee on Accreditation for the
Emergency Medical Services Professions The graduate success rate on the National
Registry of EMT Paramedic Cognitive exam is 92.3% for the 2022 annual report year.
The most recent success rate for the National Registry of EMT Paramedic Psychomotor
exam is 100%. The positive placement rate for graduates is 92.3% for the 2022 annual
report year. Positive placement is defined by the CoAEMSP as ‘Employed full or part-
time in a related field and/or continuing his/her education and/or serving in the military’.
Positive placement is measured at completion of the program. The program retention

V. Other Unit Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research.
N/A
VI. Evaluation
a) Representation of population served;
There are still underserved populations in relation to the overall demographics of the
area. See the individual program reviews for data. In general, the African-American, and
Asian population is the most underserved in comparison to county-wide data.
b) Alternative modes and schedules of delivery:
a. Canvas augmented classes. Hi fidelity skills/simulation labs.
VII. Program Vision and Goals:
a. Partnerships
i. With our satellite Paramedic Program at Mendocino College, we are abel
to enhance our partnerships with agencies associated in that area. This has
allowed our students a broader range of opportunities to do internships and
employment across California.
ii. College of the Siskiyous Paramedic Program has a very dedicated
Advisory Committee, which consists of community agencies that support
clinical and field instruction and other stakeholders in the community and
region.
iii. The EMS program contracts with several local hospitals and ambulance
agencies for clinical and internship field experience. These agencies have
been extremely supportive in helping us succeed.
b. Staffing needs
i. The program now has 1 full time faculty, and 1 full time Director assigned
to it. The full-time instructor position has been filled for the satellite and
this has helped that program grow. There is a need for a full-time faculty
position at the Weed campus program and we recommend that it comes
before the next cohort in October of 2025. This position should come with
release time to assist in the administration of the program allowing for
more involvement in the community and the allied agencies.
c. Equipment
i. We have been replacing outdated equipment and using various funding
sources to replace them. We have even partnered with agencies to receive
ambulance and equipment donations.
VIII. Progress on Prior Goals:
a. We have established the Mendocino satellite program and increased our
partnerships in that area.
b. We are starting a bachelor’s program in paramedicine in Fall of 2025.

-- 8 of 9 --

c. We have updated several classroom equipment needs and are continuing to
provide the most updated equipment to train out students.
IX. Four Year-Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement.
a. Analysis of equipment needs is ongoing, and we will continue to efficiently
improve equipment.
b. Continue to grow the programs and offerings.
c. Investigate a summer satellite agreement with Shasta College
d. Investigate an agreement with Grants Pass AMR to provide video lecture and
remote skills training.
.

-- 9 of 9 --
